text: Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon observes no dilation or ischemia in the small bowel limbs. The JJ is identified, and the limbs are examined. The Roux limb is noted to be retrocolic, retrogastric, and short, with no signs of obstruction. The biliopancreatic limb is also examined, showing no obstruction up to the ligament of Treitz.

The surgeon evaluates the gastric pouch, Peterson's defect, and the jejunojejunostomy (JJ) for internal hernias, finding no abnormalities. The common channel is inspected from the JJ to the terminal ileum, revealing no issues.
The surgeon observes laxity in the common channel, potentially allowing retrograde flow into the jejunojejunostomy (JJ). Contrast is seen moving up into the biliopancreatic limb.
The surgeon performs a plication of the common channel to the biliopancreatic limb at the site of observed laxity, using two permanent sutures to align the structures and ensure there are no kinks.
